In organized [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), an **ecclesiastical court**, also called **court Christian** or **court spiritual**, is any of certain non-adversarial [courts](/wiki/Court "Court") conducted by church-approved officials having [jurisdiction](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction") mainly in spiritual or religious matters. Historically, they interpret or apply [canon law](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law"). One of its primary bases was the *[Corpus Juris Civilis](/wiki/Corpus_Juris_Civilis "Corpus Juris Civilis")* of [Justinian](/wiki/Justinian_I "Justinian I"), which is also considered the source of the [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") legal tradition.

In the United Kingdom, secular courts that took over the functions of the ecclesiastic courts, e.g. in family law, are still known as *courts ecclesiastical* as distinct from *courts temporal*.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Medieval courts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_court&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Medieval courts")]

In the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), ecclesiastical courts had much wider powers in many areas of Europe than they did after the development of [nation states](/wiki/Nation_state "Nation state"). They held jurisdiction over not only religious matters, but also family law, equitable relief, probate, and cases involving priests, religious communities, or public heretics.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Secular courts in medieval times were numerous and decentralized: each secular division (king, prince, duke, lord, abbot or bishop as landholder, manor,[[2]](#cite_note-2) city, forest, market, etc.) could have their own courts, [customary law](/wiki/Customary_law "Customary law"), [bailiffs](/wiki/Bailiff "Bailiff") and gaols[[a]](#cite_note-4) with arbitrary and unrecorded procedures, including in Northern Europe [trial by combat](/wiki/Trial_by_combat "Trial by combat") and [trial by ordeal](/wiki/Trial_by_ordeal "Trial by ordeal"), and in England [trial by jury](/wiki/History_of_trial_by_jury_in_England "History of trial by jury in England").

Ecclesiastical courts generally followed the better regulated [inquisition](/wiki/Inquisition "Inquisition"), [accusation](/wiki/Accusation "Accusation") or [denunciation](/wiki/Denunciation "Denunciation") judicial procedures. In medieval times, they had a very wide jurisdiction including family law and [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry") disputes,[[b]](#cite_note-6) [probate](/wiki/Probate "Probate"), [equity](/wiki/Equity_(law) "Equity (law)"), defamation,[[5]](#cite_note-7) failure to observe holy days,[[6]](#cite_note-8) and cases involving priests and religious communities and individuals or public heretics.

## Anglicanism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_court&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Anglicanism")]

See also: [Canon law of the Anglican Communion](/wiki/Canon_law_of_the_Anglican_Communion "Canon law of the Anglican Communion")

### Church of England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_court&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Church of England")]

In the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), the ecclesiastical courts are a system of courts, held by authority of the [Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown"), who is *ex officio* the [Supreme Governor of the Church of England](/wiki/Supreme_Governor_of_the_Church_of_England "Supreme Governor of the Church of England"). The courts have jurisdiction over matters dealing with the rights and obligations of church members, since the 19th century limited to controversies in areas of church property and ecclesiastical disciplinary proceedings.[[7]](#cite_note-9) In England these courts, unlike common law courts, are based upon and operate along [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") procedures and [Canon law](/wiki/Canon_law_(Catholic_Church) "Canon law (Catholic Church)")-based jurisprudence.

The ecclesiastical courts formerly had jurisdiction over the [personal](/wiki/Personal_property "Personal property") [estates of deceased persons](/wiki/Estate_(law)#Inheritance "Estate (law)") to grant [probate](/wiki/Probate "Probate") or [administration](/wiki/Administration_(probate_law) "Administration (probate law)"). This jurisdiction of the Ecclesiastical Courts was transferred to the [Court of Probate](/wiki/Court_of_Probate "Court of Probate") by the [Court of Probate Act 1857](/wiki/Court_of_Probate_Act_1857 "Court of Probate Act 1857").

Offences against ecclesiastical laws are dealt with differently based on whether the laws in question involve church doctrine. For non-doctrinal cases, the lowest level of the court is the *[Archdeaconry Court](/wiki/Archdeaconry_Court "Archdeaconry Court")*, which is presided over by the local archdeacon. The next court in the hierarchy is the bishop's court, which is in the [Diocese of Canterbury](/wiki/Diocese_of_Canterbury "Diocese of Canterbury") called the *[Commissary Court](/wiki/Commissary_Court "Commissary Court")* and in other dioceses the *[consistory court](/wiki/Consistory_court "Consistory court")*. The Commissary Court is presided over by a commissary-general; a consistory Court is presided over by a chancellor. The chancellor or commissary-general must be thirty years old and either have a seven-year general qualification under the [Courts and Legal Services Act 1990](/wiki/Courts_and_Legal_Services_Act_1990 "Courts and Legal Services Act 1990") section 71 or have held high judicial office.

Specialist courts in the Province of Canterbury are the [Court of Faculties](/wiki/Court_of_Faculties "Court of Faculties"), the [Court of Peculiars](/wiki/Court_of_Peculiars "Court of Peculiars") and the [Court of the Vicar-General of the Province of Canterbury](/wiki/Court_of_the_Vicar-General_of_the_Province_of_Canterbury "Court of the Vicar-General of the Province of Canterbury"). In the northern province there is the [Court of the Vicar-General of the Province of York](/wiki/Court_of_the_Vicar-General_of_the_Province_of_York "Court of the Vicar-General of the Province of York").

The next court is the archbishop's court, which is in Canterbury called the *[Arches Court](/wiki/Arches_Court "Arches Court")*, and in York the *[Chancery Court](/wiki/Chancery_Court_of_York "Chancery Court of York")*. Each court includes five judges; one judge is common to both courts. The common judge is called the [Dean of Arches](/wiki/Dean_of_Arches "Dean of Arches") in Canterbury and the Auditor in York; he or she is appointed jointly by both archbishops with the approval of the Crown, and must either hold a ten-year [High Court](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justice "High Court of Justice") qualification under the [Courts and Legal Services Act 1990](/wiki/Courts_and_Legal_Services_Act_1990 "Courts and Legal Services Act 1990"), s 71, or have held high judicial office. Two members of each court must be clergy appointed by the Prolocutor of the Lower House of the provincial convocation.[[c]](#cite_note-10) Two further members of each court are appointed by the Chairman of the House of Laity of the General Synod;[[d]](#cite_note-11) these must possess such legal qualifications as the [Lord High Chancellor of Great Britain](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor") requires.

In cases involving church doctrine, ceremony or ritual, the aforementioned courts have no jurisdiction. Instead, the *[Court of Ecclesiastical Causes Reserved](/wiki/Court_of_Ecclesiastical_Causes_Reserved "Court of Ecclesiastical Causes Reserved")* hears the case. The Court is composed of three diocesan bishops and two appellate judges; it has jurisdiction over both of the provinces of Canterbury and York. The Court, however, meets very rarely.

Appeal from the Arches Court and Chancery Court (in non-doctrinal cases) lies to the [King-in-Council](/wiki/King-in-Council "King-in-Council"). In practice, the case is heard by the [Judicial Committee of the Privy Council](/wiki/Judicial_Committee_of_the_Privy_Council "Judicial Committee of the Privy Council"), which includes present and former [Lords Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor"), a number of [Lords of Appeal](/wiki/Judicial_functions_of_the_House_of_Lords "Judicial functions of the House of Lords") and other high judicial officers. The [King-in-Council](/wiki/King-in-Council "King-in-Council") does not have jurisdiction over doctrinal cases from the [Court of Ecclesiastical Causes Reserved](/wiki/Court_of_Ecclesiastical_Causes_Reserved "Court of Ecclesiastical Causes Reserved"), which instead go to an *ad hoc* [Commission of Review](/wiki/Commission_of_Review "Commission of Review"), composed of two diocesan bishops and three Lords of Appeal (who are also members of the Judicial Committee).

The operation of such courts was not without cost. For instance, in 1843, the gross fees, salaries, emoluments of the judges, deputy-judges, registrars, deputy-registrars, and all other officers in the ecclesiastical courts of England, Wales and Ireland, amounted to £120,513, of which £101,171 was the figure for England.[[8]](#cite_note-12)

**Commissions of Convocation** were appointed by the Upper House of the [Convocation](/wiki/Convocation "Convocation") of Canterbury or of York to try a bishop for an offence (except for an offence of doctrine). Both Convocations make the appointment if an archbishop is prosecuted. This would comprise four diocesan bishops and the Dean of the Arches. They were discontinued by the Clergy Discipline Measure 2003.

#### Guernsey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_court&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Guernsey")]

The [Ecclesiastical Court in Guernsey](/wiki/Courts_of_Guernsey "Courts of Guernsey") predates its first written evidence from the 13th Century. Its powers have been reduced over the centuries but it still meets weekly to prove wills and to grant marriage licences.[[10]](#cite_note-14)

### Episcopal Church in the United States of America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_court&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Episcopal Church in the United States of America")]

Ecclesiastical courts in the American [Episcopal Church](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "Episcopal Church in the United States of America") have jurisdiction only over disciplinary cases involving clergy and are divided into two separate systems: one for trials of bishops (at the level of the national Episcopal Church) and the other for trials of priests and deacons (at the level of the diocese for original jurisdiction and at the provincial level for appeals).[[11]](#cite_note-TECConstIX-15) (At least one diocese, however, [Diocese of Minnesota](/wiki/Episcopal_Diocese_of_Minnesota "Episcopal Diocese of Minnesota"), has provided in its canons for a court with broader jurisdiction over a wide range of canonical issues, although such a court has not yet been implemented by the convention of that diocese.[[12]](#cite_note-Minnesotacanon-16)) In each disciplinary case, two courts are provided, one for trials and one for appeals. When a charge is first made, it is brought before an initial review committee (similar to a [grand jury](/wiki/Grand_jury "Grand jury") in secular criminal law) whose job is to determine when a case should be brought and to supervise the Church Attorney who acts as a sort of [prosecutor](/wiki/Prosecutor "Prosecutor").[[13]](#cite_note-TECCanonIV.3-17)

Courts and procedure for trials of bishops are provided for by the Canons of the [General Convention](/wiki/General_Convention "General Convention") (the triennial legislative body of the national church).[[14]](#cite_note-TECCanonIV.5-6-18) There is one Court for the Trial of a bishop, composed of nine bishops (though there have been proposals to include lay persons and lower clergy in this court). Appeals are heard by the Court of Review for the Trial of a bishop, also comprising nine bishops. The Constitution of the national Episcopal Church provides that this court must be composed only of bishops.[[11]](#cite_note-TECConstIX-15)

For priests and deacons, initial trial is held by an ecclesiastical court established by the diocese in which the cleric is canonically resident.[[15]](#cite_note-TECCanonIV.4.(A)-19) Appeals are taken to the Court of Review for the Trial of a Priest or Deacon, one of which is established in each of the nine [provinces](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_province "Ecclesiastical province") of the Episcopal Church (a province is a geographic combination of dioceses).[[16]](#cite_note-TECCanonIV.4.(B)-20) Dioceses have some discretion about the procedure and membership for the ecclesiastical court, but most rules and procedure are established church-wide by the national canons. Trial courts are made up of lay persons and of priests or deacons, with the clergy to have a majority by one. The various courts of review comprise one bishop, three priests or deacons, and three lay persons.

Since the 18th century the Constitution of the national Episcopal Church has permitted the creation of a national Court of Appeal, which would be "solely for the review of the determination of any Court of Review on questions of Doctrine, Faith, or Worship".[[11]](#cite_note-TECConstIX-15) No such court has ever been created, though proposals have occasionally been made to establish the [House of Bishops](/wiki/House_of_Bishops "House of Bishops") itself as such a court.

### Anglican Church in North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_court&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Anglican Church in North America")]

Trials of clergy within the [Anglican Church in North America](/wiki/Anglican_Church_in_North_America "Anglican Church in North America") take place according to canons of individual dioceses, while trials of bishops are conducted by a seven-member Court for the Trial of a Bishop, comprising three bishops, two priests and two [confirmed](/wiki/Confirmation "Confirmation") adult members.[[17]](#cite_note-TLC_-_June_2025_-_ACNA-21) Cases are referred to the court by a board of inquiry appointed by the [archbishop](/wiki/List_of_archbishops_of_the_Anglican_Church_in_North_America "List of archbishops of the Anglican Church in North America"). The board decides whether a [presentment](/wiki/Presentment "Presentment"), which must be signed by at least three diocesan bishops or by a sufficient number of clergy and laity from within the accused bishop's diocese, offers [probable cause](/wiki/Probable_cause "Probable cause") to present the bishop for trial.[[17]](#cite_note-TLC_-_June_2025_-_ACNA-21)[[18]](#cite_note-RNS_-_2023_-_ACNA-22)

Unlike disciplinary hearings of the Episcopal Church, the court's proceedings may be held virtually and may be closed to the public and to media.[[17]](#cite_note-TLC_-_June_2025_-_ACNA-21) The ACNA canons establish an [adversarial system](/wiki/Adversarial_system "Adversarial system") in which a prosecutor represents the province and the accused (who is presumed innocent) may obtain representation. A majority of the court members is required for a verdict to be reached on a charge.[[19]](#cite_note-TLC_-_August_2025_-_ACNA-23) A bishop found guilty may appeal to the seven-member Provincial Tribunal. As of 2025, the court has heard two cases, one involving [Todd Atkinson](/wiki/Todd_Atkinson "Todd Atkinson") and the ongoing [ecclesiastical trial of Stewart Ruch](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_trial_of_Stewart_Ruch "Ecclesiastical trial of Stewart Ruch").[[18]](#cite_note-RNS_-_2023_-_ACNA-22)

The tribunals of the modern-day [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") are governed by the [1983 Code of Canon Law](/wiki/1983_Code_of_Canon_Law "1983 Code of Canon Law") in the case of the Western Church ([Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church")), and the [Code of Canons of the Eastern Churches](/wiki/Code_of_Canons_of_the_Eastern_Churches "Code of Canons of the Eastern Churches") in the case of the [Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches") (Byzantine, Ukrainian, Maronite, Melkite, etc.). Both systems of canon law underwent general revisions in the late 20th century, resulting in the new code for the Latin Church in 1983, and the compilation for the first time of the Eastern Code in 1990.

Cases normally originate in the tribunal of the [particular church](/wiki/Particular_church "Particular church") (i.e. the [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") or [eparchy](/wiki/Eparchy "Eparchy")) of the parties to the case. This tribunal in canon law is called the *tribunal of first instance*. The bishop of the church possesses the power to judge for his church; however, since the bishop has many different duties in his diocese, most cases are handled by [judges](/wiki/Judge "Judge") whom he appoints, led by a priest known as the [judicial vicar](/wiki/Judicial_vicar "Judicial vicar") or *[officialis](/wiki/Officialis "Officialis")*.

A single judge can handle normal contentious and penal cases. A college of at least three judges, however, must try cases involving an excommunication, the dismissal of a [cleric](/wiki/Cleric "Cleric"), or the annulment of the bond of marriage or of sacred ordination (can. 1425 §1). The bishop can assign up to five judges to a case that is very difficult or important (can. 1425 §2). Otherwise, the judicial vicar assigns cases to the judges and, in those cases which require three or more judges, presides over the panel or assigns one of his assistant judicial vicars to preside, if there are any. The judicial vicar and the assistant judicial vicars must be [priests](/wiki/Priest "Priest") with [doctorates](/wiki/Doctorate "Doctorate") or at least [licentiates](/wiki/Licentiate_(degree) "Licentiate (degree)") in [canon law](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law"). The other judges need only be [clerics](/wiki/Cleric "Cleric") with licentiates, but the [episcopal conference](/wiki/Episcopal_conference "Episcopal conference") can permit members of the [laity](/wiki/Laity "Laity") with the same academic qualifications to serve as judges on a panel.

There are other officers of the tribunal. The promoter of justice, for instance, is a canon lawyer whose job is to represent the diocese as the prosecutor in penal cases and who also can intervene in contentious cases if they concern the "public good", acting as a watch dog for the people of the diocese. Another important officer is the defender of the bond, another canon lawyer whose job is to present reasons to the tribunal why a [marriage](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") is valid in cases of alleged nullity and why an [ordination](/wiki/Ordination "Ordination") is valid in the rare cases of alleged nullity of [Holy Orders](/wiki/Holy_Orders "Holy Orders"). The tribunal also has notaries who swear in witnesses and commit their testimony to writing. Parties in a case have the right to appoint an advocate who can argue for them at the tribunal. If a person cannot afford an advocate, the tribunal can assign one to them free of charge.

Unlike courts of [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") tradition, ecclesiastical tribunals do not follow the [adversarial system](/wiki/Adversarial_system "Adversarial system"). Based on the same Roman [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") that is behind much European law, the procedure of a canonical court is more akin to the [inquisitorial system](/wiki/Inquisitorial_system "Inquisitorial system"), with the judges leading the investigation. As a general rule, the defendant has the favorable presumption of law, which means that the defendant will win by default unless a majority of the judges is convinced with moral certainty of the petitioner's case (can. 1608).[[20]](#cite_note-24) This presumption also applies in penal cases (can. 1728). There are few exceptions to this rule; in those cases, the burden shifts to the defendant.

Some matters cannot be introduced at the diocesan level and can only be introduced before the following:

* Appellate tribunal for the diocese: cases against the diocese itself or an institution represented by the diocesan bishop
* [Roman Rota](/wiki/Roman_Rota "Roman Rota"): cases against the heads of religious orders, cases against dioceses or church institutions that are immediately subject to the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"), and non-penal cases against bishops
* The [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope") himself: any case where a [cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholicism) "Cardinal (Catholicism)"), [Eastern rite](/wiki/Eastern_Rite_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Rite Catholic Churches") [patriarch](/wiki/Patriarch "Patriarch"), [papal legate](/wiki/Papal_legate "Papal legate"), or [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") is a defendant and any penal case involving a [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop").

### Appeal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_court&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Appeal")]

The [appellate tribunal](/wiki/Appellate_court "Appellate court") is known as the *tribunal of second instance*.[[21]](#cite_note-25) Normally the second instance tribunal is the tribunal of the [metropolitan bishop](/wiki/Metropolitan_bishop "Metropolitan bishop"). In the case where the appeal is from a first instance decision of the metropolitan's own tribunal, the appeal is taken to a court which the metropolitan designated with approval of the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"), usually another nearby metropolitan, thus ensuring that appeals from one diocese are never heard by the same diocese. As an example, a case in the [Diocese of Springfield, Massachusetts](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Springfield_in_Massachusetts "Roman Catholic Diocese of Springfield in Massachusetts"), would be appealed to the tribunal of the [Archdiocese of Boston](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Boston "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Boston"), but a case originating in the Archdiocese of Boston would be appealed to the tribunal of the [Archdiocese of New York](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_New_York "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of New York"), by agreement between the archbishops of New York and Boston.

Some cases are automatically appealed (for instance, when a [marriage was found to be null](/wiki/Annulment_(Catholic_Church) "Annulment (Catholic Church)") prior to Pope Francis’ 2015 reforms). The appealing party does not need to appeal to the metropolitan; the party can instead appeal to the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"), in which case the [Roman Rota](/wiki/Roman_Rota "Roman Rota") would hear the case in the second instance. If the case was before the Rota in the first instance, then a different panel of the Rota hears it in the second instance.

With the exception of cases regarding personal status, if the first instance and second instance tribunals agree on the result of the case, then the case becomes *[res judicata](/wiki/Res_judicata "Res judicata")* and there is no further appeal. If they disagree, then the case can be appealed to the Roman Rota, which serves as the *tribunal of third instance*. The Rota is a court of fifteen judges called *auditors* who take cases in panels of three and serve as the final arbiters of most cases.

There is no appeal from a court case that the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope") has decided personally.

### Other tribunals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_court&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Other tribunals")]

The [Roman Curia](/wiki/Roman_Curia "Roman Curia") has two other tribunals which either deal with specialized cases or which do not deal with cases at all. The first is the [Apostolic Signatura](/wiki/Apostolic_Signatura "Apostolic Signatura"), a panel of five [cardinals](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholicism) "Cardinal (Catholicism)") which serves as the highest court in the Roman Catholic Church. Normal cases rarely reach the Signatura, the exception being if a party appeals to the Pope and he assigns the case to them or if the Pope on his own initiative pulls a case from another court and gives it to them. The court mainly handles cases regarding the use of administrative power, including penal cases which were decided using executive instead of judicial power, which is the usual case. It also handles disputes between [dicasteries](/wiki/Dicasteries "Dicasteries") and other tribunals over jurisdiction, complaints that a Rotal decision is null and should be retried, and matters regarding advocates and inter-diocesan tribunals.

There is normally no right of appeal from the decision of the Apostolic Signatura (can. 1629 #1); however, laypersons and clerics have, on rare occasions, convinced the Pope to hear their case afterwards. This is usually reserved for cases where they are facing [excommunication](/wiki/Excommunication_(Catholic_Church) "Excommunication (Catholic Church)") or some other form of severe censure, such as the loss of the right to teach [theology](/wiki/Catholic_theology "Catholic theology") or to administer the [sacraments](/wiki/Sacraments_of_the_Catholic_Church "Sacraments of the Catholic Church"). Facing [censure](/wiki/Censure_(Catholic_canon_law) "Censure (Catholic canon law)"), a theologian and priest got [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") to hear his case and even asked the Pope to alter his own decision, though the Pope did not reverse the ruling in either case.

The other tribunal is the [Apostolic Penitentiary](/wiki/Apostolic_Penitentiary "Apostolic Penitentiary"). This tribunal has no jurisdiction in what is known as "the external forum", meaning cases and events which are publicly known, only matters of the "internal forum", which involve entirely confidential and secret matters, including (but not limited to) what is confessed in the [Sacrament of Penance](/wiki/Sacrament_of_Penance "Sacrament of Penance"). It primarily deals with cases that arise only within the confessional and which by their nature are private, confidential or whose facts are secret. Such cases are normally brought before the court by a person's confessor, who writes up the relevant facts of the cases, but only what is absolutely necessary, using standardized Latin pseudonyms. The confidentiality of the person, and the priest's absolute obligation to preserve the secrecy of the Sacrament of Penance, are still in force in such cases. This court, under the authority of the Cardinal Major Penitentiary, who acts in the Pope's name, answers the confessor and empowers him to impose a penance and lift a penalty. For instance, the act of desecrating the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist_in_the_Catholic_Church "Eucharist in the Catholic Church") is one which incurs an automatic excommunication for the person who so acts (an excommunication from the moment of the act, which no court need actually meet to impose), and the power to lift this excommunication is reserved by the Pope to himself. Should this person then approach a priest in confession, repentant, and explain his act and the fact that he acted in secret, the confessor would write to the tribunal laying out the simplest outline of facts, keeping the person's identity secret, and would most likely be empowered to lift the excommunication and impose some private act of penance on the person.

## Eastern Orthodoxy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_court&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Eastern Orthodoxy")]

The dioceses of many Eastern Orthodox denominations, such as the [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church"), have their own ecclesiastical courts. In addition, the Russian Orthodox Church has a General Ecclesiastical Court with jurisdiction throughout the Russian Orthodox Church, as well as the Court of the Bishops’ Council which serves as the court of final appeal.[[22]](#cite_note-26) Under the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), Russian Orthodox ecclesiastical courts held jurisdiction over cases of [adultery](/wiki/Adultery "Adultery"), [incest](/wiki/Incest "Incest"), [bestiality](/wiki/Bestiality "Bestiality"), and [blasphemy](/wiki/Blasphemy_law "Blasphemy law"). They also held responsibility for divorce, which could only be obtained in cases of adultery.[[23]](#cite_note-27)

The Greek Eastern Orthodox churches have ecclesiastical courts. For example, the [Archdiocese of Thyateira and Great Britain](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Thyateira_and_Great_Britain "Archdiocese of Thyateira and Great Britain") under the spiritual jurisdiction of the [Patriarchate of Constantinople](/wiki/Patriarchate_of_Constantinople "Patriarchate of Constantinople") has such courts for determining whether to grant divorce after the State has.[[24]](#cite_note-28)

## Methodism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_court&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Methodism")]

The Judicial Council is the highest court in the [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church"). It consists of nine members, both laity and clergy, elected by the [General Conference](/wiki/General_Conference_(Methodism) "General Conference (Methodism)") for an eight-year term. The ratio of laity to clergy alternates every four years.[[25]](#cite_note-29) The Judicial Council interprets the *[Book of Discipline](/wiki/Book_of_Discipline_(United_Methodist) "Book of Discipline (United Methodist)")* between sessions of General Conference, and during General Conference the Judicial Council rules on the constitutionality of laws passed by General Conference. The Council also determines whether actions of local churches, annual conferences, church agencies, and bishops are in accordance with church law. The Council reviews all decisions of law made by bishops[[26]](#cite_note-30) The Judicial Council cannot create any legislation; it can only interpret existing legislation. The Council meets twice a year at various locations throughout the world. The Judicial Council also hears appeals from those who have been accused of chargeable offenses that can result in defrocking or revocation of membership.

## Mormonism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_court&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Mormonism")]

Main article: [Church membership council](/wiki/Church_membership_council "Church membership council")

In [the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints"), church courts are formally known as [church membership councils](/wiki/Church_membership_council "Church membership council"). Church courts consider possible membership withdrawal or restriction based on a member's violation of church standards. Any [stake](/wiki/Stake_(Latter_Day_Saints) "Stake (Latter Day Saints)"), [ward](/wiki/Ward_(LDS_Church) "Ward (LDS Church)"), or [mission](/wiki/Mission_(LDS_Church) "Mission (LDS Church)") of the church may convene a church court.

The [Common Council of the Church](/wiki/Common_Council_of_the_Church "Common Council of the Church")—which has only been convened twice—is a church court which has the authority to remove a [president of the church](/wiki/President_of_the_Church_(LDS_Church) "President of the Church (LDS Church)") or one of his counselors in the [First Presidency](/wiki/First_Presidency_(LDS_Church) "First Presidency (LDS Church)") for misbehavior.

## Presbyterianism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_court&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Presbyterianism")]

The [Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.)](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(U.S.A.) "Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.)") has Permanent Judicial Commissions for each synod, presbytery and the [General Assembly](/wiki/General_assembly_(Presbyterian_church) "General assembly (Presbyterian church)") of the denomination, all of which are elected by members and are composed of ministers and elders subject to its jurisdiction. The PJC of the General Assembly consists of one member from each of the sixteen synods which compose the denomination and the PJC has original jurisdiction over remedial cases and appeals.
